title: Indigenous Grief Tending and the Anger Inside
dek: *Grief and anger live in the same nervous system pocket, and Indigenous communities have known this for millennia. What happens when we stop treating anger as the enemy and start asking what loss it's trying to protect? This piece explores body-based grief practices that honor the sacred heat inside your chest and the lineages that never forgot how to tend it with fire and water both.*
bodyMarkdown:
TL;DR. Indigenous grief practices treat anger as sacred information about boundaries and belonging, not a problem to eliminate. These traditions understand that unmetabolized grief often surfaces as rage, and they offer specific somatic rituals for tending both. When we learn to track anger back to its grief roots, we reclaim an embodied wisdom that Western psychology is only beginning to map.

You have been told that anger is the opposite of peace. That if you were truly evolved, truly healed, you would transcend this heat in your throat and the tightness behind your ribs. That spiritual maturity means a kind of pleasant neutrality, a life lived in beige tones where nothing ever rises to meet you with teeth bared. But the Māori, the Lakota, the Quechua, the Tlingit, and hundreds of other Indigenous peoples across continents have a different story. They say anger is grief's bodyguard. And grief, when it cannot move, when it has no ceremony to hold it, no fire to burn through it, no songs to carry it, turns hard and hot in the body. It becomes the thing we call rage. ## The Nervous System Keeps the Tally Your body does not distinguish between the loss of a loved one and the loss of a way of life. Between the death of your grandmother and the death of the language she spoke. Between personal trauma and collective erasure. Peter Levine's somatic experiencing work shows us that trauma lives in the incomplete survival responses, the fight or flight that never got to finish. In our experience, anger often marks the place where grief froze mid-reach. The fist that wanted to protect something precious. The roar that wanted to say "this matters" when everything around you whispered that it did not. The NIH overview of anger and the autonomic nervous system maps what Indigenous healers have tracked for generations: anger activates the sympathetic branch, raising heart rate, flooding the bloodstream with cortisol and adrenaline, preparing the body for action. But here is what Western medicine is slower to name: that same arousal pattern can be the body's way of saying "I am still here, I still care, I refuse to go numb." Anger, in this light, is not pathology. It is proof of attachment. You cannot grieve what you did not love. The Dagara people of West Africa hold a cosmology where grief is considered one of the essential nutrients for community health. Sobonfu Somé taught that ungrieved loss becomes a toxin that poisons relationships, decision-making, and the body itself. When grief has no outlet, no witness, no container, it calcifies. And calcified grief looks a lot like chronic anger. The kind that does not resolve. The kind that sits in your jaw at night and follows you into every conversation. ## What Gets Lost When Grief Goes Underground Thich Nhat Hanh spoke often about anger as a crying baby. You do not punish an infant for wailing. You pick it up. You investigate. Is it hunger? Cold? Fear? In the same way, anger in the body is a signal, not a sin. It says: something here needs tending. But most of us were not raised in cultures that teach this kind of listening. We were taught to exile the anger, to apologize for it, to "manage" it with breathing techniques that bypass the message entirely. Indigenous grief practices work differently. They assume that the body already knows what it needs. That the shaking, the wailing, the keening, the pounding of fists into earth are not breakdowns but breakthroughs. When the Yolngu people of Australia lose a loved one, they engage in loud, embodied mourning that can last for days. The whole community participates. No one is alone with their loss. No one is told to "be strong" or "move on." The anger, the despair, the howling, all of it is welcomed as part of the sacred process of letting go. Compare that to the typical Western funeral: subdued, brief, contained. We get three days of bereavement leave if we are lucky. We are expected to return to productivity, to normalcy, as if the shape of the world has not fundamentally changed. And when the grief has nowhere to go, it does not disappear. It finds a home in your shoulders, your lower back, your digestion, your reactivity. Over the years I've seen this pattern again and again: people who come to the work of anger wondering why they snap at their partners, why they feel a constant low hum of irritation, why nothing ever feels quite right. And when we start to track it somatically, when we slow down and ask the body what it is holding, we almost always find a grief that was never given permission to move. ## Grief as Fire Medicine Many Indigenous traditions understand that different emotions require different elements for their transformation. Water for sorrow. Earth for grounding. Air for clarity. And fire for rage. The Mexica (Aztec) temazcal ceremony, a sweat lodge ritual, uses intense heat to purge what the body is ready to release. You sit in the dark, in the steam, in temperatures that make your skin scream, and you let the fire do its work. You do not control it. You do not manage it. You let it burn through you. This is not the same as "venting" anger, which research shows often reinforces the neural pathways of reactivity. This is ceremony. This is witnessing. This is the difference between throwing a tantrum and offering your rage to something larger than yourself. The fire does not judge. It just transforms. And when you emerge from the temazcal, you are not the same. Something has been metabolized. Something has been composted. If you are reading this and thinking you do not have access to a temazcal or a grief ritual led by an Indigenous elder, you are right. And you are also not without options. You can still work with fire as a metaphor and a physical presence. Light a candle. Write what you are angry about on paper. Speak it aloud. Then burn the paper and watch the smoke carry it upward. This is not cultural appropriation. This is working with an archetypal element that belongs to no one and everyone. Across the articles we've published on this site, we return again and again to the necessity of embodied practice. Anger that stays only in the mind never completes its cycle. ## The Body Knows How to Grieve Thomas Moore, in his work on the soul's need for depth, reminds us that not everything needs to be "fixed." Some things need to be honored. Mourned. Sat with. The soul does not operate on the same timeline as productivity culture. It does not care about your five-year plan. It cares about what is true. And sometimes what is true is that you are furious because you lost something that mattered. A relationship. A version of yourself. A future you were counting on. A heritage that was stolen. The Anishinaabe have a practice called the "Feast for the Dead," where food is prepared and offered to the spirits of those who have passed. The living eat together, tell stories, laugh, and cry. The dead are not separate. They are still part of the family. And the grief is not a problem to solve. It is a relationship to maintain. This is so foreign to a culture that wants to "get over" loss, to "find closure," to "move forward." But the body does not move forward in a straight line. It spirals. It returns. It remembers. One of the simplest and most powerful practices you can do right now, in the next five minutes, is this: place one hand on your heart and one hand on your belly. Close your eyes. Take three slow breaths. Then ask your body, "What are you grieving?" Do not answer with your mind. Let your body respond. Maybe it is a tightness in your throat. Maybe it is a heat behind your eyes. Maybe it is a memory that surfaces without words. Just notice. Do not fix. Do not solve. Just be with what arises. This is the beginning of Indigenous-informed grief work: believing that your body is not broken, it is speaking. ## Anger as Boundary Medicine In many Native American traditions, anger is understood as a sacred no. It is the part of you that knows when a line has been crossed. When a treaty has been broken. When a promise has been betrayed. Colonization did not just steal land. It stole the right to be angry about the theft. It pathologized resistance. It called grief "depression" and rage "aggression" and then medicated both into silence. But anger, when it is connected to grief, is not aggression. It is clarity. It is the part of you that says, "This is not okay. This will not stand. I will not pretend this is fine." The Haudenosaunee (Iroquois) Confederacy had systems for conflict resolution that honored anger as information. Councils would meet. Grievances would be spoken. And the anger was not shamed. It was tracked back to its source. What boundary was violated? What grief is underneath? How do we restore balance? When you start to see anger this way, as a messenger rather than a monster, the whole landscape shifts. You stop trying to eliminate it and start trying to understand it. You ask: what is this anger protecting? What does it love so much that it would rise up with claws out? And almost always, the answer is something tender. Something that was hurt. Something that is still hoping, against all odds, to be seen. For some, supporting the body's mineral balance can help stabilize the nervous system's response to emotional intensity. [Pure Encapsulations Potassium Magnesium Citrate (180 caps)](https://www.amazon.com/dp/B0017I29HG?tag=spankyspinola-20) offers a clean formulation that many find helpful during periods of heightened stress and grief work, though always consult your healthcare provider before adding supplements. ## Ritual as the Container for What Cannot Be Spoken Words are not always enough. Sometimes the grief is too big for language. Sometimes the anger is pre-verbal, from a time before you had the capacity to name what was happening to you. This is where ritual becomes essential. The Yup'ik people of Alaska have drum dances where the whole body becomes the prayer. The rhythm, the movement, the collective presence, all of it creates a field where the unspeakable can be held. You do not need a community of hundreds to create ritual. You can create it alone. You can create it with one trusted friend. The elements are simple: intention, repetition, witness, and closure. You decide what you are tending. You do a specific action (lighting a candle, speaking a name, placing a stone, moving your body in a particular way). You let yourself be seen, even if only by the walls. And then you mark the end. You bow. You thank the process. You close the circle. In my own practice, I have found that anger softens when it is given a shape. When it is not just a formless storm inside but something with edges, something that can be placed on an altar or whispered into a fire or danced into the ground. The anger does not go away. But it becomes workable. It becomes sacred. It stops being the enemy and becomes the guide. Some traditions use herbs to support the body's processing of heat and inflammation. [Plum Flower Four Marvel Teapills (Si Miao Wan, 200 ct)](https://www.amazon.com/dp/B004ZH5ROI?tag=spankyspinola-20) is a classical Chinese formula used for clearing damp-heat, which in traditional medicine can correlate with the kind of stuck, smoldering anger that lives in the tissues. ## The Practice of Witnessing Without Fixing One of the most radical acts in a culture obsessed with solutions is to simply witness. To sit with someone in their anger and not try to talk them out of it. To hold space for grief without offering platitudes. The Maori practice of tangi, a communal mourning ritual, is built on this principle. People come. They cry. They wail. They speak to the dead. And no one says, "It will be okay." No one says, "They are in a better place." They just stay. They just witness. They just let the grief be as big as it needs to be. If you have never been witnessed in your anger, if you have never had someone sit with you while you raged and not flinch, not fix, not make it about them, then you do not yet know how healing that can be. You do not yet know that your anger is not too much. That your grief is not a burden. That you are not broken for feeling what you feel. This is what [the fire toolkit](/fire-toolkit) is built around: practices that honor the intelligence of your anger and give it room to breathe. Not techniques to suppress or "manage" it, but ways to let it move through you and transform into something generative. ## When the Anger Belongs to Your Ancestors Sometimes the rage you feel is not just yours. It is inherited. It is the unmetabolized trauma of your lineage. The Indigenous concept of "blood memory" speaks to this: that our bodies carry the experiences of those who came before us. That genocide, slavery, displacement, violence, all of it lives in the nervous system and gets passed down until someone in the line is ready to tend it. This is not metaphor. Epigenetic research is beginning to show that trauma can alter gene expression in ways that affect subsequent generations. The anger you feel when someone dismisses your pain, when someone tells you to "get over it," when someone erases your history, that anger might be older than you. It might be your great-grandmother's anger. Your great-great-grandfather's grief. And it is asking you to be the one who finally gives it a voice. You do not have to carry it alone. You can offer it to the earth. You can speak it to the wind. You can find others who are doing this work and witness each other. [Theoraclelover.com](https://theoraclelover.com) offers additional resources for exploring the spiritual dimensions of emotional work, particularly when the feelings you are navigating feel larger than one lifetime. Some people find that calming the nervous system with scent can create a gentle entry point into grief work. [Plant Therapy Lavender Essential Oil (30 mL)](https://www.amazon.com/dp/B0069SQQ2I?tag=spankyspinola-20) can be diffused or applied topically (diluted) to support relaxation during emotional processing, though it is not a substitute for the deeper work of letting the anger speak. ## Questions Readers Ask About Anger and Grief ### Can I really grieve something I never had? Yes. You can grieve the childhood you deserved and did not get. The safety you needed and did not receive. The culture that was taken from your lineage before you were born. Grief is not only about loss. It is about the gap between what was and what could have been. Your body knows that gap. It feels it as absence, as longing, as a quiet rage that nothing was ever the way it should have been. Tending that grief is not self-pity. It is honesty. ### How do I know if my anger is "healthy" or "toxic"? Anger becomes toxic when it has no outlet, no witness, no transformation. When it just cycles in your head and hardens into resentment or explodes into harm. Healthy anger moves. It has a beginning, a middle, and an end. It tells you something about your boundaries or your values, and then it shifts. If your anger feels stuck, chronic, or if it is damaging your relationships and you cannot seem to stop, that is a sign it needs tending. Not shaming. Tending. Often through the body, often with support, often by tracking it back to the grief it is protecting. [The assessments hub](/assessments) can help you identify patterns in your emotional landscape and point you toward the specific practices that might serve you. ### Is it cultural appropriation to use Indigenous grief practices if I am not Indigenous? This is a nuanced question. If you are taking a closed ceremony, claiming it as your own, commodifying it, or stripping it of its context, yes, that is appropriation. But if you are learning from Indigenous teachers with respect, if you are acknowledging the source, if you are working with universal elements like fire, water, earth, and breath in ways that honor their sacredness without claiming ownership, that is different. Many Indigenous elders teach that these practices are meant to be shared, with integrity. The key is humility, credit, and a willingness to learn the history and the pain that shaped these traditions. Your anger and your grief are real. The practices that help you tend them do not have to be invented from scratch, but they do have to be approached with reverence. ## What the Anger Wants You to Remember The anger is not asking you to burn the world down. It is asking you to remember what you love. To remember what mattered so much that its loss still echoes in your bones. To remember that you are not neutral, not numb, not past caring. You are alive. You are here. And something in you refuses to pretend that what was taken does not matter. The grief underneath your anger is not a pit you will fall into and never climb out of. It is a doorway. And on the other side is a kind of tenderness that only comes from having loved something enough to mourn it. Indigenous grief practices do not promise you that the pain will go away. They promise you that it will have meaning. That it will connect you to something older and wiser than the small, scared self that just wants the hurt to stop. They promise you that your anger, when it is honored, when it is given fire and water and song, will become medicine. Not just for you. For everyone who comes after. If you are hungry for [more articles in this journal](/articles) that explore the relationship between embodied practice and emotional healing, you will find a growing archive of voices who refuse to let this work stay abstract. ---
Written by **The Oracle Lover**, independent essayist on anger, somatics, and embodied spirituality. Last updated May 28, 2026. In our work on indigenous grief tending and the anger inside, we keep returning to one quiet truth: the body has been telling you something for a long time, and it deserves a thoughtful listener. The Anger Practice is a companion journal to [theoraclelover.com](https://theoraclelover.com), and every piece here is written with you in mind.
